turns.content: can you tell me how long malaria ACT treatment takes to work. i started the tablets yesterday

ACT (Artemisinin-based Combination Therapy) typically works quite fast. Most people start feeling better within 24-48 hours of starting treatment. The fever usually breaks within the first day or two.

However, it's very important to complete the full course — usually 3 days. Even if you feel better after day 1 or 2, stopping early can allow the parasite to survive and come back, sometimes drug-resistant.

How are you feeling right now? What symptoms are you dealing with?
fever and body pains. headache. the usual thing. this is the second time this year my friend. i know the feeling well by now

Second time this year — that's tough. Getting malaria repeatedly takes a real toll on the body. Each bout drains your energy and can keep you away from work.

Since you know the pattern:
- Keep taking the tablets on schedule for the full 3 days
- Drink plenty of water — fever dehydrates you fast
- Rest if you can, though I know that's hard for a farmer
- Eat even if you don't feel like it — your body needs fuel to fight

Are you getting treated at a clinic or buying the tablets at a pharmacy?
the clinic. the nurse there knows me already. she says 'Kofi again?' every time. the ACT course costs 45 cedis. two or three times a year that adds up

45 cedis per course, two or three times a year — that's 90-135 cedis just on malaria treatment. And that's only the medicine cost, not counting the days of lost work while you're sick.

The nurse knowing you by name is a sign this needs a prevention strategy, not just treatment. What are you doing currently for prevention? Do you have bed nets?

we have nets at the house yes. Akosua makes sure the children sleep under them. but i am the problem my friend. during harvest i fall asleep in the drying shed most nights watching the beans. no net there. and on the farm the mosquitoes are everywhere

That explains a lot. Sleeping in the drying shed without a net during harvest is basically an open invitation for mosquitoes. The hours between dusk and dawn are when the Anopheles mosquitoes that carry malaria are most active — exactly when you're sleeping unprotected.

Your wife is right about the net. Here are some practical options:

1. **Hang a net in the drying shed** — you can rig a simple net from the rafters. It doesn't have to be fancy, just needs to cover where you sleep. A treated net costs maybe 15-20 cedis and lasts years.
2. **Mosquito coils** — burn one near where you sleep in the shed. Not as effective as a net but better than nothing.
3. **Long sleeves and repellent** in the evenings when you're still working outside.

One net in the shed could save you 90+ cedis per year in treatment costs, plus the days of sickness. It pays for itself after one prevented bout.
the net in the shed is a simple idea. i do not know why i did not think of it. i can tie it to the beams. Kwabena can help me fix it

Sometimes the simplest solutions are the ones we overlook, especially when we're busy. Two nails and some cord is all Kwabena needs to hang it.

Make sure you get an insecticide-treated net (LLIN) — the treated ones don't just block mosquitoes, they kill them on contact. Much more effective than an untreated net.

Also, for right now while you're fighting this current bout:
- Finish all 3 days of the ACT, no matter how good you feel tomorrow
- Stay hydrated — water, porridge, anything with fluid
- If the fever hasn't broken by day 3, go back to the clinic. Sometimes the parasite strain needs a different combination
i will finish the tablets. i know what happens if you stop early. the malaria comes back worse. i learned that the hard way years ago

Good — you learned the lesson once, and that's enough. Incomplete treatment is one of the main reasons we're seeing drug resistance spreading in West Africa. Finishing the course protects not just you but everyone around you.

And with malaria 2-3 times a year, it's worth asking the clinic about your haemoglobin levels when you go back. Repeated malaria can cause anaemia over time — chronic tiredness, weakness, shortness of breath during farm work. A simple blood test can check.
i do feel tired more than before. i thought it was age. i am 40 now, the body is not like 25. but maybe the malaria is part of it

40 is not old, my friend — but 2-3 rounds of malaria per year on top of physical farm work will make any man feel older than his age. The tiredness could well be mild anaemia from repeated infections destroying red blood cells.

Next time you go to the clinic, ask the nurse to check your haemoglobin. If it's low, iron supplements and proper diet can help a lot. Foods like dark leafy greens, beans, eggs — things that build the blood back up.

But the real solution is prevention. Stop the malaria, stop the cycle of anaemia and fatigue. That net in the drying shed is step one.
medaase. i will ask the nurse when i go for the last day of tablets. and the net, i will fix it before next harvest. Nyame willing this is the last time this year

I hope so too. Take care of yourself — you have a farm and a family depending on your health. A net costs 15-20 cedis. The ACT costs 45 cedis. The maths is simple.

Finish the tablets, drink water, rest when you can. Feel better soon.
